coredata.dc:title: Solvothermal synthesis of g-C3N4 nanosheets modified carbon quantum dots for enhanced photocatalytic degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Linxia
Lu, Yining
Xu, Jingjing
Tao, Feifei
Liang, Pengfei
coredata.dc:description: The incorrect disposal of industrial waste water has caused widespread concern of researchers due to its serious harm to people's health and ecological environment. Photocatalytic technology is widely used for wastewater purification owing to its simple operation, no secondary pollution and clean energy. In this study, carbonaceous composites were constructed by combining graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via hydrothermal method. The size of CQDs was about 8 nm, distributing over the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets, and the existence of their interface interaction was confirmed. The photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) is considered to appraise the photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4/CQDs composites under visible light. The obtained composites display better photocatalytic activity than the pure g-C3N4 nanosheets. The obtained g-C3N4/CQDs composite (CN-20) from 20 mL of the CQDs solution (∼1 mg mL−1) and 20 mg of g-C3N4 nanosheets displays the excellent photo-degradation efficiency and its apparent rate constant (k app) is 4.0 times that of g-C3N4 nanosheets. In addition, CN-20 exhibits almost negligible influences of real environmental (pH value, inorganic anions, humic acid and source of water) on the degradation of MO, indicating potential in practical application. Furthermore, the g-C3N4/CQDs composite shows the enhanced cycle stability and the postulated mechanism of MO photocatalytic degradation is illustrated in detail.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4)
Visible-light-responsive photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of some factors on photocatalytic performance

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, grant No. 51372154 and 20901051 ), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. LY21B010005 and the Project of Shaoxing University (No. 2022LG003 ). The authors thank Yongwei Li from Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaoxing University for the help in the synthesis of the photocatalysts.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of CQDs (a, b), g-C3N4 nanosheets (c, d) and CN-20 (e, f).
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore diameter distribution (inset) of g-C3N4 nanosheets (a) and CN-20 (b).
XPS survey spectra (a), C 1s (b) and N 1s (c) XPS spectra of g-C3N4 nanosheets and CN-20.
UV–vis spectra of various photocatalysts (a) and Tauc plots (b, c) of g-C3N4 nanosheets (b) and CN-20 (c).
PL spectra of various photocatalysts at the excitation wavelength of 300 nm (a) and the up-conversion luminescence spectra of CQDs (b).
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MO over various catalysts and (b) ln(C 0/C t) versus t curves.
Effect of initial pH value on the MO degradation over CN-20.
(a) Five consecutive loops of the MO degradation over CN-20 and g-C3N4 nanosheets under irradiation and (b) XRD curves of CN-20 after 5 cycles.
Trapping experiments of active species for the MO degradation over CN-20.
Possible photo-degradation mechanism of MO over the g-C3N4/CQDs composite.
The k app value for various catalysts under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Solvothermal synthesis of g-C3N4 nanosheets modified carbon quantum dots for enhanced photocatalytic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaoxing University, Zhejiang, 312000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaoxing University, Zhejiang 312000, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, 201210, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201210, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaoxing University, Zhejiang, 312000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The incorrect disposal of industrial waste water has caused widespread concern of researchers due to its serious harm to people's health and ecological environment. Photocatalytic technology is widely used for wastewater purification owing to its simple operation, no secondary pollution and clean energy. In this study, carbonaceous composites were constructed by combining graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via hydrothermal method. The size of CQDs was about 8 nm, distributing over the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets, and the existence of their interface interaction was confirmed. The photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) is considered to appraise the photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4/CQDs composites under visible light. The obtained composites display better photocatalytic activity than the pure g-C3N4 nanosheets. The obtained g-C3N4/CQDs composite (CN-20) from 20 mL of the CQDs solution (∼1 mg mL−1) and 20 mg of g-C3N4 nanosheets displays the excellent photo-degradation efficiency and its apparent rate constant (k app) is 4.0 times that of g-C3N4 nanosheets. In addition, CN-20 exhibits almost negligible influences of real environmental (pH value, inorganic anions, humic acid and source of water) on the degradation of MO, indicating potential in practical application. Furthermore, the g-C3N4/CQDs composite shows the enhanced cycle stability and the postulated mechanism of MO photocatalytic degradation is illustrated in detail.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, with the increase of the world population and the acceleration of industrialization, many severe problems and challenges have been brought to the society, such as energy shortage and environmental pollution [1,2]. According to the investigation, water pollution is one of the most serious environmental problems endangering peoples’ health. Many technologies such as biochemical technology [3], electrochemical technology [4] and ozone technology [5] are used to deal with the water pollution problems, but the high cost and cumbersome operations limit their application. In recent years, photocatalysis technology that can use sunlight to degrade various pollutants has attracted more attention [6,7]. Based on these advantages of photocatalysis technology, such as simple operation, no secondary pollution, clean energy and low cost, a large number of researchers have invested in this field. Various photocatalysts have been synthesized successfully, such as metal oxide catalysts (TiO2, ZnO, WOx and CeO2) [8–11], sulfide photocatalysts (CdS, Ni2S3 and MoS2) [12–14] and noble metal modified semiconductors (Ag2CO3, Ag/AgCl and Pt–TiO2) [15–17].

In order to improve the photocatalytic performance, it is an effective method to construct the non-metallic photocatalysts with π-conjugated graphitic planes structure [18–21], which possesses the enhanced surface area for active sites and the ability to separate photo-generated electron–hole pairs [22]. To date, graphene (GO), fullerene (C60) and g-C3N4 and so on, as the carbonaceous materials, have aroused much concern in the photocatalysis. Yuan et al. [23] constructed the g-C3N4/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) heterojunction by π-π hybridization for the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light. It was found that introduction of rGO could promote the segregation of the photo-generated charges and improve the photo-degradation activity. Bai et al. [24] constructed C60-g-C3N4 composite with the higher photo-degradation activity and photocurrent response than pure g-C3N4. Kang et al. designed a metal-free carbon nitride/carbon quantum dots (g-C3N4/CQDs) for the enhanced water splitting [25].

Among various carbonaceous materials, the g-C3N4 as a non-metallic semiconductor has many remarkable merits, such as dependable stability, low-cost and easy synthesis in large quantities, especially the adjustable energy level and relatively narrow energy band [26]. So g-C3N4 can be widely used in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from water splitting [27], CO2 reduction [28] and degradation of contaminants [29] and so on. However, bulk g-C3N4 still has some shortcomings that cannot be ignored, for example, the weak visible-light absorbance, little specific surface areas and the high binding of photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) [30,31]. Many strategies have been developed to overcome these drawbacks, for instance, designing the g–C3N4–based composite with other semiconductors [32–34], precious metal deposition [35–37] and surface modification of co-catalysts [38].

As a new fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with particles size mostly below 10 nm, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) consisting of amorphous to nanocrystalline nuclei and typical graphite-like sp2 or diamond-like sp3 hybrids, have been widely used to enhance capabilities in the field of photocatalysis [39]. The CQDs have many advantages, such as less toxicity, excellent photochemical reliability, up-conversion photoluminescence and adjustable fluorescence emission [40–42]. Especially, the celerity migration of photo-excited charges is more easily because the CQDs can act as both electron donor and electron acceptor. Moreover, the unique up-conversion luminescence property of the CQDs that long-wavelength light can be converted into short-wavelength light, can enlarge the sunlight absorption and boost the photocatalytic ability [43]. A lot of heterojunctions combined with CQDs have been constructed and applied in some domain of biotechnology, sensors, photochemical stabilization, chemiluminescence and electrocatalysis. For instance, Chandra et al. [44] synthesized nitrogen/sulfur-co-doped fluorescent CQDs by one-pot hydrothermal method as a selective nano-probe owing to its excellent blue emission under UV light. Ma et al. [45] prepared the CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles on dendritic fibrous nanosilica (DFNS), which exhibited excellent photocatalytic antibacterial activity. Zhao et al. [46] constructed the TiO2/g-C3N4/CQDs for the enhanced photocatalytic degradation of gaseous benzene. All these researches show that the loading of CQDs is conducive to the formation of heterojunction with g-C3N4, the effective separation of photo-generated carriers and the broadened light response, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the loading of CQDs is expected to be an excellent photocatalyst in wastewater treatment.

Herein, the pyrolysis method and hydrothermal method were used to prepare the g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs, respectively, which were further utilized to construct the g-C3N4/CQDs heterojunction through the simple solvothermal method. The existence of CQDs has no obvious effect on the morphology and structure of g-C3N4 nanosheets, but its light absorption capacity is improved significantly. The MO degradation was obviously enhanced compared with pure g-C3N4 under visible light. Moreover, the photo-degradation mechanism of MO over g-C3N4/CQDs was suggested based on a range of optical tests and trapping experiments of active species. The research is anticipated to render the reference for synthesis of non-metallic photocatalysts, which may play a vital role in water pollution treatment in future.

g-C3N4 nanosheets: The g-C3N4 nanosheets can be prepared successfully by using melamine as the precursor with the modified method [1]. Firstly, 9.0 g of melamine was heat treated at 550 °C in a crucible with a lid for 4 h with the rate of temperature rise at 5 °C·min−1. The sample was cooled to 20 °C and washed with deionized water several times after reaction. Finally, dry it at 60 °C for 4 h in an oven. The powder obtained was g-C3N4 nanosheets.

CQDs: A typical hydrothermal method was applied to prepare the CQDs [47]. 3.0 g of citric acid was dissolved completely in 15 mL of deionized water with the ultrasound assistance. Then the reactants were transferred to autoclave with the temperature rising to 180 °C for 5 h. Then the obtained solution was dialyzed with deionized water for 4–5 d to acquire the CQDs solution (∼1 mg mL−1).

g-C3N4/CQDs composites: Typically, the g-C3N4/CQDs composites were prepared by the simple solvothermal method. 20 mg of g-C3N4 nanosheets and a proper amount of the CQDs solution were mixed under the action of ultrasound and then heated at 120 °C for 3 h in autoclave. When the g-C3N4 nanosheets were mixed with 5, 10, 20, 40 and 60 mL of the CQDs solution, respectively, the corresponding composites were marked as CN-5, CN-10, CN-20, CN-40 and CN-60, respectively.
The characterization, photocatalytic tests and trapping experiments of reactive species of the photocatalysts are described in the supporting information (S1, S2 and S3), respectively.

The morphologies of CQDs, g-C3N4 nanosheets and CN-20 were characterized by HRTEM in Fig. 1 . In Fig. 1a the CQDs show a zero-dimensional structure with the size of about 8 nm, which can be further proved by Fig. 1b. Based on the observation of the enlarged HRTEM image, the lattice stripes could not be found, indicating the poor crystallinity of the CQDs, which is consistent with XRD research. From Fig. 1c and d, the graphite-like layered structure of g-C3N4 can be obtained with the smooth surface and is tightly stacked with less obvious stratification, which agglomerates into nearly massive pieces [48]. The CN-20 sample displays that a good deal of quantum dots are well distributed on the surface of nanosheets due to the modification of CQDs (Fig. 1e), proving that the product is composed of CQDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets. Due to the modification of the CQDs, the g-C3N4 nanosheets show good dispersion, which is helpful to obtain large specific surface area and more active sites in photocatalysis. Moreover, the lattice diffraction fringes are not clearly surveyed in Fig. 1f, suggesting the weak crystallinity of the composite, which is consistent with those of the CQDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets.

The crystal type of g-C3N4 nanosheets and the g-C3N4/CQDs composites were studied with XRD (Fig. 2 ). According to the literatures [49–51], g-C3N4 nanosheets have two typical characteristic peaks (13.1° and 27.5°), which are attributed to (100) and (002) crystal planes of hexagonal g-C3N4 (JCPDS No. 87-1256), respectively. The weak peak at 13.1° is ascribed to the tri-s-triazine structure units of the g-C3N4 plane and the strong peak at 27.5° is connected with the accumulation of conjugated aromatic hydrocarbons [51]. The g-C3N4/CQDs composites display the similar XRD curve to g-C3N4 nanosheets, indicating that the introduction of the CQDs does not destroy the crystal structure of g-C3N4 nanosheets. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks display a slightly reducing intensity with the increasing CQDs content, which is possibly attributed to the strong interface interaction from the restrained periodic potential field of oriented melons [50]. There are no other diffraction peaks in the g-C3N4/CQDs composites, indicating that the CQDs have poor crystallinity or amorphous structure.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were further illustrated to test the porous properties of catalysts (Fig. 3 ). The results reveal that the isotherms of the CN-20 and g-C3N4 nanosheets could be appointed to type IV with a typical H3-type hysteresis loop confirming the existence of mesoporous structures [52]. Besides, the BET specific surface area of g-C3N4 nanosheets and CN-20 are 8.34 and 8.70 m2/g, respectively. The CN-20 exhibits the slightly higher surface area, which might be owed to the loading of the CQDs with the small size on the g-C3N4 nanosheets. Meanwhile, there are a plenty of mesopores and macropores in the range of 2–100 nm for g-C3N4 nanosheets and CN-20 from the pore size distribution (insets of Fig. 3). In Comparison to g-C3N4 nanosheets, the CN-20 sample has more pores concentrated at less than 10 nm, resulting in much higher specific surface area. And the enlarged surface area of CN-20 can gain the more active sites and is conducive to its photocatalytic ability.

XPS technology is used to confirm the chemical composition and electronic structure of the g-C3N4 nanosheets and CN-20 (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4a, the C and N elements could be detected in g-C3N4 and CN-20, respectively. For g-C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. 4b), the main C 1s peaks at 284.8 and 288.1 eV correspond to C–C bonds and sp2 -hybridized carbon atoms (C=N or C≡N) existing in aromatic ring [53], respectively. There are three N 1s peaks in g-C3N4 (Fig. 4c). The peak positioned at 398.7 eV belongs to the sp2 hybridized aromatic nitrogen bonded graphitic carbon (C–N=C). The peak at the binding energy of 399.9 eV is assigned to tertiary nitrogen (N-(C)3) groups [54]. The weak peak at 401.2 eV is assigned to amino group (C–N–H) bounding to partial condensation of the poly (tri-s-triazine) ring [55]. The CN-20 sample has the typical C 1s and N 1s spectra similar to pure g-C3N4 nanosheets, and there is a slight shift in CN-20 due to the introduction of CQDs. It further confirms the interface interaction between g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs [53], illustrating that the CN-20 photocatalyst is not a mechanical mixture of the g-C3N4 and CQDs.

The absorption property of light is one of the main factors to evaluate photocatalytic ability. The UV–vis spectra of the prepared photocatalysts within the range of 200–800 nm are shown in Fig. 5 . Overtly, the g-C3N4/CQDs composites have the UV–vis curves similar to the g-C3N4 nanosheets from Fig. 5a. Compared with g-C3N4 nanosheets, the g-C3N4/CQDs composites show a slight red-shift of absorption edge, indicating that the loading of CQDs can expand the visible-light response range, similar to the literature research [28]. Furthermore, the absorption intensity of visible light displays the trend of first increasing and then decreasing with the addition of CQDs (inset of Fig. 5a). And the CN-20 sample displays the significantly enhanced absorption at the wavelength of 450–800 nm, indicating the higher absorption capacity of visible light [56]. Based on the calculation of the Kubelka–Munk function from Tauc's plots (Fig. 5b and c), the band gaps (E g) are 2.79 and 2.63 eV for g-C3N4 nanosheets and CN-20, respectively. The low band gap energy of CN-20 is attributed to the CQDs doping, which changes the electron configuration of g-C3N4 nanosheets [35]. More visible light can be assimilated and utilized because of the low band gap of the CN-20, which improves the utilization of solar energy.

Another important factor in evaluating the photocatalytic properties is the separation efficiency of photo-generated electrons and holes, which can be characterized with photoluminescence (PL) spectrum [57]. It is known that the lower PL intensity means the higher electron-hole separation [58]. As shown in Fig. 6 a, a strong peak at 465 nm is clearly observed from the band-band PL phenomenon [35]. The PL peak intensity first goes down and then up as the CQDs continues to be added to the C3N4 nanosheets (inset of Fig. 6a), and the CN-20 composite shows the slightly lowest PL peak than other photocatalysts, which represents that the proper amount loading of CQDs can promote the charge separation. The up-conversion luminescence signifies that the semiconductor can be excited by the light of low energy and emit the light of high energy, which is contrary to Stokes’ law. As shown in Fig. 6b, the CQDs display the obvious up-conversion luminescence excited by the visible light at the wavelength range of 500–750 nm. So the g-C3N4/CQDs composites have the expanded light response range and more visible light can be utilized to produce the electrons and holes. It's helpful for the markedly promotion of the photocatalytic ability of pollutants.

The MO solution was acted as a model dye contaminant to illustrate the photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4/CQDs composites. Fig. 7 a shows the degradation of the MO under visible light of 120 min. Only 3.6% of MO was removed in the absence of photocatalysts, indicating that self-degradation of MO hardly occurs. The MO molecules could be rapidly removed with the help of photocatalysts. When the CN-20 was added to MO solution, the removal efficiency was only 6.5% without irradiation. The above control experiments suggest that both photocatalysts and irradiation are indispensable factors to improve the degradation activity of MO. It can be observed from Fig. 7a that compared with primitive g-C3N4 nanosheets, the g-C3N4/CQDs composites display the increasing photocatalytic properties of MO and the removal of MO shows the change trend of boosting first and decreasing with the raises of the CQDs content. The CN-20 has the best photocatalytic performance with the MO removal of 83% under the irradiation of 120 min. The more CQDs in the composite decrease the photo-degradation of MO due to the fact that the excess CQDs in the composite could assemble each other or become new recombination centers, thereby leading to recombination of the photo-generated electrons-holes pairs [59]. It is concluded that the proper amount of CQDs in composites can accelerate the charge separation, which eventually causes the excellent activity in the photocatalytic reaction.

In order to further study the degradation performance of MO by various catalysts, the pseudo first-order Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) mode was used to describe the MO degradation process. It can be described by the equation (eq. (1)) [60].
(1)
ln
(
C 0
C t
)
=
k
a p p
t
where k app is the apparent rate constant (min−1), the initial concentration of MO and the actual concentration of MO at the irradiation time t are labeled as C 0 and C t, respectively. Fig. 7b presents the fitting kinetic curves of ln(C 0 /C t) and t for the MO degradation over various samples. Table 1 lists the k app values for various catalysts. The g-C3N4/CQDs composites display that the k app value first goes up and then down with the increasing amount of the CQDs, and the k app value of pure g-C3N4 is the lowest. And the CN-20 sample has the largest k app value of 0.0219 min−1 that is 4.0 times that of pure g-C3N4 nanosheets. It is further confirmed that g-C3N4 nanosheets decorated by CQDs can have the excellent photocatalytic performance.

To deep explore the photocatalytic performance of the prepared photocatalysts. Rhodamine B (RhB) and methylene blue (MB) were used as aim pollutants to further investigate the photocatalytic universality of compounds under visible light. As shown in Fig. S4a, only 40.3% of RhB was removed by pure g-C3N4 under irradiation of 120 min. Under the same conditions, about 80.1% of RhB could be degraded by CN-20. Besides, it can be seen from Figured S4b that unadulterated g-C3N4 could remove only 24.3% of MB, while the removal of MB could reach 79.1% over CN-20.

In addition to investigating the photocatalytic degradation of dyes, other photocatalytic reactions have been tested in this work, such as H2O2 production and H2 production by visible light. As shown in Fig. S4c, only about 2.08 μmol L−1 of H2O2 is produced over the g-C3N4 nanosheets under visible light irradiation of 120 min. However, the H2O2 production assisted by CN-20 is significantly improved and reaches 29.12 μmol L−1, which is about 14 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4. Similar results can be found in the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction (Fig. S4d). The CN-20 composite shows the remarkable enhancement in evolution rate of H2 (40.2 μmol h−1) that is about 8 times as much as pure g-C3N4 (5.0 μmol h−1). The above results prove that the modification of CQDs could effectively enhance photocatalytic ability of g-C3N4. In a word, the construction of CN-20 heterojunction is conductive to prevent the recombination of photo-excited charges and exhibit excellent photocatalytic universality [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of some factors on photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH value about the degradation of MO by CN-20 was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 8 , the MO degradation was carried out with the initial pH value from 2.0 to 10.0, which revealed that the degradation efficiency of MO was strongly dependent on the pH value [62]. The CN-20 has the excellent photo-degradation at the acidic condition that is conducive to the adsorption of pollution target on the surface of the catalysts.

In practical wastewater treatment, inorganic anions and dissolved organic matter (DOM) are often detected. The effects of these substances on degradation of MO were studied in Figs. S5a and S5b. The existence of inorganic anions (Cl− and NO3 −) has hardly effect on photocatalytic degradation of MO over CN-20. However, the SO4 2− has slightly influence on the degradation of MO over CN-20, mainly due to the inhibition of h+ by SO4 2− [63]. The result is consistent with the subsequent experiments on capture of active species. Meanwhile, the addition of CO3 2− reduced the photocatalytic degradation of MO over CN-20. This is because that H+ in the solution is combined with CO3 2−, leading to the increased pH value. It can be seen from Fig. S5b that the existence of humic acid (HA), a typical representative of DOM, can promote the degradation of MO at low concentration (5 mg L−1), but inhibit the photocatalytic reaction at high concentration (10～20 mg L−1), and the inhibitory effect increases with the increase of HA concentration. As shown in Fig. S5b, when the concentration of HA was 5 mg L−1 and 20 mg L−1, the final removal efficiency of MO over CN-20 was 89.2% and 65.2%, respectively. This is due to the fact that low concentration of HA can be used as a photosensitizer to promote the generation of active species, which can improve the degradation effect of pollutants. At the same time, HA at high concentration will be adsorbed on the surface of photocatalyst and occupy its active site, reducing the contact between pollutants and the active site of the catalyst [64]. Both of these actions are present simultaneously in the photocatalytic process. Besides, HA is negatively charged in water, which has the repulsion to anionic pollutant MO, leading to the situation that low concentration HA promotes the MO removal.

Furthermore, considering the real dye wastewater treatment over the photocatalyst, MO will be added to all kinds of water to get the concentration of 10 mg L−1, such as deionized water, tap-water, river water and sea water. As shown in Fig. S5c, the degradation efficiency of MO by CN-20 in deionized water, tap-water, river water and sea water were 83.0%, 81.9%, 80.8% and 84.2%, respectively. The research proved that MO could be photo-degraded evidently even though the sources of water were different. Therefore, the influence of water sources on the degradation of MO over CN-20 could be ignored, which helps to the practical application of the g-C3N4/CQDs photocatalyst.

The photocatalytic stability is also an indispensable factor in the study of catalyst materials [65]. Five loops of the MO degradation over g-C3N4 nanosheets and CN-20 were tested under the same experimental conditions. The recovered catalyst was washed with deionized water and dried for next recycling after each test. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the degradation efficiency of MO decreased by 1.0% and 7.5% after five recycles for the CN-20 and pure g-C3N4 nanosheets, respectively. Otherwise, the crystal phase of the used CN-20 was characterized by XRD to investigate its stability. As shown in Fig. 9b, the used CN-20 after five consecutive cycles shows the similar XRD to the fresh CN-20, indicating the stability of crystal structure. Moreover, the content of carbon and nitrogen elements of CN-20 before and after the reaction is detected by elemental analysis (EA). Based on the EA results, the content of the CQDs in CN-20 is determined to be 50.0 wt% and 49.6 wt% before and after the photocatalytic degradation, respectively. There is almost no loss of the CQDs during the photocatalytic reaction, indicating good stability. This also explains the good photocatalytic activity after five cycles. In a word all the results display that CN-20 has outstanding reusability and stability, which may be due to the potent interaction between CQDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets.

To understand the mechanism of MO degradation reaction, the active species were captured by trapping experiment. As shown in Fig. 10 , at the presence of EDTA-2Na, the MO degradation is decreased evidently. Only 19.5% of MO is removed under the irradiation of 120 min, indicating h+ plays a leading function in the MO degradation. When BQ is added to the reaction solution, the degradation of MO is inhibited obviously, suggesting that ·O2 − is also an important active species in the reaction. To further discover the effect of ·O2 −, the additional experiment was performed in nitrogen atmosphere. About 48% of MO is removed under the irradiation of 120 min. It means that O2 molecules involve in the photocatalytic reaction and promote the formation of ·O2 −. In addition to EDTA-2Na and BQ, the degradation of MO is slightly decreased with the introduction of TBA, which suggests that ·OH has a little effect on the photocatalytic reaction [63].

To further explore the probable photocatalytic mechanism of MO degradation over the g-C3N4/CQDs composite, the position of conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) needs to be further confirmed. Based on the Mott-Schottky (M − S) curve (Fig. S6a), the flat band potential of the g-C3N4 nanosheets is −0.68 V vs. Ag/AgCl (pH = 7), which can be calculated −0.48 V vs. normal hydrogen electron (NHE) [66]. According to the VB-XPS spectrum (Fig. S6b), the VB of the g-C3N4 nanosheets is 1.29 V vs. NHE. Therefore, the CB of g-C3N4 nanosheets is −1.50 V vs. NHE.

Based on the above experimental results, a probable photocatalytic mechanism is proposed in Scheme 1 . First, g-C3N4 nanosheets can be excited to generate electrons and holes once exposing to visible light. Then the photo-generated electrons can be transmitted from VB to CB, leaving the holes in the VB of g-C3N4 nanosheets with strong oxidation. Second, the electrons can jump to the CQDs as the electron acceptor, which can inhibit the recombination of photo-excited charges thus prolonging their lifetime. Third, due to the up-conversion luminescence, more photo-generated charges can be produced by exciting g-C3N4 nanosheets under the broadened range of visible light. Finally, the CB potential of g-C3N4 nanosheets is −1.50 eV vs. NHE, which is more negative than E O2/O2 − (−0.33 V vs. NHE.). So O2 can be reduced to generate because of the existence of the photo-generated electrons in the CB [66]. The ·O2 − on the CB of g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs can effectively degrade the MO molecules. Meanwhile, the VB potential of g-C3N4 nanosheets is 1.29 eV vs. NHE, more negative than the E ·OH/H2O, meaning that the H2O molecules cannot be oxidized to produce ·OH radicals, which is consistent with the trapping experimental results of active species. So the holes in VB directly oxidize the MO molecules. Therefore, the g-C3N4/CQDs composites exhibit excellent photocatalytic performance, which is attributed to the effective separation and transfer of the photo-generated electrons and holes.

Herein, the g-C3N4/CQDs composites as the non-metallic photocatalyst were successfully synthesized by the simple solvothermal method. The CQDs are uniformly dispersed on the g-C3N4 nanosheets, which inhibits the aggregation of g-C3N4 nanosheets, thus increasing the specific surface area. Compared with the pure g-C3N4 nanosheets, CN-20 composite shows the low band gap and the weak PL peak, indicating the broadened absorption range of light and the inhibited recombination of charges. The g-C3N4/CQDs composites exhibit the improved photocatalytic activity of MO under visible light irradiation and the large k app value of the MO degradation over CN-20 is 4.0 times that of g-C3N4 nanosheets. Meanwhile, CN-20 also has outstanding degradation for the removal of RhB and MB under visible light. The trapping experiments of active species showed that h+ and ·O2 − played an important role in the photo-degradation of MO by CN-20. These results reveal that the g-C3N4/CQDs composites show the outstanding photocatalytic performance and cyclic stability. The design and construction of the g-C3N4/CQDs composite proves a feasible route to synthesize the carbonaceous photocatalyst and is valuable for the practical photocatalytic application in wastewater treatment.

Linxia Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Writing-Original Draft; Yining Lu: Data Curation, Writing-Original Draft preparation; Jingjing Xu: Data Curation, investigation; Feifei Tao: Conceptualization, Funding Acquisition, Resources, Superivision, Writing-Review & Editing; Pengfei Liang: Validation, Data Curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, grant No. 51372154 and 20901051), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. LY21B010005 and the Project of Shaoxing University (No. 2022LG003). The authors thank Yongwei Li from Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaoxing University for the help in the synthesis of the photocatalysts.
